inference"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2022-05-12T00:41:15Z","updated_at":"2024-10-01T03:25:23Z","closed_at":"2024-10-01T03:25:22Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"_edit:_ See program `toverflow7.jl` and its discussion in a later comment.  `toverflow7` is 28 lines long instead of the 140 of `toverflow5.jl`.  The runs-ok version now is `toverflow8`, obtained by commands `A=toverflow7;B=toverflow8;sed -e 's/if 1<2/if 1>2/' $A.jl > $B.jl; chmod u+x $B.jl`. <strike>Program `toverflow5.jl` appears below.   Program `toverflow3.jl` is made from `toverflow5.jl` by the command ```sed -e 's/if 1<2/if 1>2/' toverflow5.jl > toverflow3.jl```.</strike>  These programs illustrate that at the moment, seemingly-minor changes (using one print instead of two, writing to file instead of `stdout`) trigger a few error messages for Julia 1.9.0-DEV, or thousands of traceback and error messages for Julia 1.7.2, which is disconcerting.

This may be related to problems reported in issues [type inference stackoverflow #43050](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43050) and [StackOverflow Type Inference Error #44852](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44852).  I also posted about this in discourse.julialang.org at [Stack overflow in type inference… julia 1.9.0-DEV](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/stack-overflow-in-type-inference-julia-1-9-0-dev/80694?u=jjiw).

`toverflow3` runs ok via Julia 1.7.2 (which is `julia` on my system, see `versioninfo()` at end of post) and via Julia 1.9.0-DEV.485 (which is `julia-latest`, ditto), writing 1 line to `stdout` and 2 lines to `outP00`.

Command `julia-latest toverflow5.jl` produces 4 identical 2-line error-message sets and then produces expected results.  The message sets are like:
```
Internal error: stack overflow in type inference of (::toverflow.var"#findMore#7"{Array{Array{Float64, 1}, 1}, Array{Tuple, 1}, Array{Array{Float64, 1}, 1}, Array{Int32, 1}, Array{Int32, 1}, Array{Float64, 1}, Int64, Float64})(Int64).
This might be caused by recursion over very long tuples or argument lists.
```

Running 1.7.2, the command `julia toverflow5.jl` produces four similar groups of 19028 error lines each.  For example, `grep -n 'Internal error' over-script-1.7.2b` shows:
```
3:Internal error: encountered unexpected error in runtime:
19031:Internal error: encountered unexpected error in runtime:
38059:Internal error: encountered unexpected error in runtime:
57087:Internal error: encountered unexpected error in runtime:
```
where `over-script-1.7.2b` is result of running `toverflow5` within `script over-script-1.7.2b`.

Note, the difference between `toverflow3` and `toverflow5` is that the former uses two `printfmt` calls to print the same things that `toverflow5` uses one `printfmt` for.  The single-character difference between the files (as shown in the following output from `diff toverflow3.jl toverflow5.jl`) makes an if-test `false` for `toverflow3` and `true` for `toverflow5`.  The `false` case of the `if` contains 2 x 8-argument  `printfmt`s while  the `true` case has one 14-argument  `printfmt`.
```
58c58
<     if 1>2           # sed changes < to > for other version
---
>     if 1<2           # sed changes < to > for other version
```
Note, if the output to `outP00` is instead written to `stdout` (ie, if `outP00` isn't opened/written to/closed) the stack overflow message goes away, otherwise I would not have file-writing included in the problem's working example.  Of course that makes the argument list to the many-argument `printfmt` have one less argument; however, when writing to `stdout` I ran a test with 50 arguments in the `printfmt`, as follows, and it worked ok:  
```
printfmt("({:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d} {:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d},{:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d}{:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d} {:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d},{:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d} {:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d}, {:2d}, {:5.2f}, {:5.2f}, {:5.2f}, {:5.2f}, [{:5.2f}, {:5.2f}, {:5.2f}], [{:5.2f}, {:5.2f}, {:5.2f}], ", DRP.RPnumber, n, n+2, n+6, n+7, n+3, n, n+12, n+16, n+17, n+13, n, n-2, n-6, n-7, n-3, n, n-12, n-16, n-17, n-13, n, n+2, n+6, n+7, n+3, n, n+12, n+16, n+17, n+13, n, n-2, n-6, n-7, n-3, n, n-12, n-16, n-17, n-13, r, s, raxi, zaxi, c[1],c[2],c[3], nnnv[1],nnnv[2],nnnv[3])
```
suggesting number of arguments is not the whole story.

Note, to some extent the number of error messages that print can be decreased or increased by moving the `]#` characters earlier or later within the `cots = ...` array.

Note, if the recursion-call `findMore(level+1)` is commented out, then `toverflow3` results are unchanged, while `julia-latest toverflow5.jl` runs with only one error-message pair:
```
Internal error: stack overflow in type inference of toverR().
This might be caused by recursion over very long tuples or argument lists.
```
while  `julia toverflow5.jl` runs with about 19030 error message lines before it produces ok results.

For example, we recently saw this error:
```julia

```
I truncated the message, but that `Tuple{}` goes on for a _very long time._

However, we currently can't figure out where in our code that is coming from!
It would be helpful to also report two locations, I think:
1. The source location (file:line) of the dynamic dispatch that caused this type inference invocation
2. The source location (file:line) of the method being inferred.

In this case, the function being inferred (2.) would be in `Base` somewhere, so not all that helpful. But maybe sometimes it would be.
But (1.) would be _extremely_ useful, so that we could figure out where this was coming from!

In fact, even better would be to actually dump the stack trace... if that was possible it would be a huge improvement. Thanks!

The exception is reported here:

------

{"action":"closed","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50603","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50603/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50603/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50603/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50603","id":1812629020,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5sCoYc","number":50603,"title":"Could we fix "Stack overflow in type inference" by moving compilation to another Task?","user":{"login":"NHDaly","id":1582097,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1ODIwOTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1582097?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly","html_url":"https://github.com/NHDaly","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":184008807,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxODQwMDg4MDc=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/domain:multithreading","name":"domain:multithreading","color":"fbca04","default":false,"description":"Base.Threads and related functionality"},{"id":371587497,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzE1ODc0OTc=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/design","name":"design","color":"e99695","default":false,"description":"Design of APIs or of the language itself"},{"id":421532175,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw0MjE1MzIxNzU=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/compiler:inference","name":"compiler:inference","color":"bfdadc","default":false,"description":"Type inference"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2023-07-19T19:48:55Z","updated_at":"2024-10-01T03:29:05Z","closed_at":"2024-10-01T03:29:05Z","author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"There have been several issues reporting `Stack overflow during type inference`, which can crash a julia process entirely.

Here are a few of them:
- https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49237
- https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45286
- https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48813
- Plus we've seen this a bunch in production, but haven't filed an issue for all of them.

One of the most common places this occurs is if we're in a try/catch, and we're trying to _handle the exception we've caught_, but it turns out that we're right up near the edge of the stack, and the exception we caught is a `StackOverflowError`, and so then in the catch block when trying to handle it (log it, switch on its type, report it to our users, abort the current operation, etc), we end up hitting a `stack overflow during type inference` since we're calling code for the first time that hasn't been compiled yet for this specific exception.

But it could also happen during normal julia operation: If you're about to dispatch to a function that would push you over the stack limit, but before you call, you need to compile it, you can hit this error.

-------

It seems like this situation could be solved by having _separate Task(s)_ where we do compilation, and switching over to them when we need to compile something. The benefit would be that we would avoid stack overflows during inference, which really screw up the runtime. The downside is that this task switch would likely make compilation more expensive.

Maybe first we need to work on performance of task switching?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50603/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":1,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50603/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55952","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55952/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55952/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55952/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55952","id":2559085520,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6YiIvQ","number":55952,"title":"Improve performance of depth-limited type printing in stack traces","user":{"login":"charleskawczynski","id":1880641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE4ODA2NDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1880641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski","html_url":"https://github.com/charleskawczynski","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/charleskawczynski/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-10-01T12:30:57Z","updated_at":"2024-10-01T12:37:17Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Can we improve the performance of depth-limited type printing in stack traces? Here is an example where printing truncated types takes quite a while with a highly nested struct:

```julia
Base.@kwdef struct Nested{A,B}
    num::Int = 1
end
nest_val(na, nb, ::Val{1}) = Nested{na, nb}()
nest_val(na, nb, ::Val{n}) where {n} = nest_val(Nested{na, nb}, Nested{na, nb}, Val(n-1))
nest_val(na, nb, n::Int) = nest_val(na, nb, Val(n))
nest_val(n) = nest_val(1, 1, n)
foo1(t::Nested) = error("oops")
foo2(t::Nested) = foo1(t)
foo3(t::Nested) = foo2(t)
foo4(t::Nested) = foo3(t)
foo5(t::Nested) = foo4(t)
foo6(t::Nested) = foo5(t)
foo7(t::Nested) = foo6(t)
foo8(t::Nested) = foo7(t)
foo9(t::Nested) = foo8(t)
foo10(t::Nested) = foo9(t)
NV = nest_val(20); # be careful with changing 20 to larger
foo10(NV) # errors, takes ~45 seconds
```

I haven't looked at the implementation in a while, but I imagine that this could be related to #55807.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55952/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55952/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2385668097","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55952#issuecomment-2385668097","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55952","id":2385668097,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6OMmgB","user":{"login":"nsajko","id":4944410,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ5NDQ0MTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4944410?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko","html_url":"https://github.com/nsajko","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-01T12:37:16Z","updated_at":"2024-10-01T12:37:16Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Try the latest nightly, xref #55575.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2385668097/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

::Integer)`","user":{"login":"nsajko","id":4944410,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ5NDQ0MTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4944410?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko","html_url":"https://github.com/nsajko","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":171885699,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNzE4ODU2OTk=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/domain:maths","name":"domain:maths","color":"93755D","default":false,"description":"Mathematical functions"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-10-01T21:55:34Z","updated_at":"2024-10-01T21:55:35Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"```julia
using Test
@testset "`^(::AbstractIrrational, ::Integer)`" begin
    @testset "c: $c" for c ∈ (ℯ, π, MathConstants.γ, MathConstants.φ, MathConstants.catalan)
        let e = -1 
            @test c ≈ (c^e)^e
        end    
    end    
end
```

```none
Test Summary:                        | Pass  Error  Total  Time
`^(::AbstractIrrational, ::Integer)` |    1      4      5  1.6s
  c: ℯ                               |    1             1  0.0s
  c: π                               |           1      1  1.2s
  c: γ                               |           1      1  0.1s
  c: φ                               |           1      1  0.1s
  c: catalan                         |           1      1  0.1s
ERROR: Some tests did not pass: 1 passed, 0 failed, 4 errored, 0 broken.
```

Among the `Irrational` constants, only `ℯ` works as a base for exponentiation here, I guess because that case gets forwarded to `exp`.

I guess the fix is to define a method something like the following:

```julia
^(c::AbstractIrrational, n::Integer) = AbstractFloat(c) ^ n
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55961/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55961/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

I wonder if this is intentional. stage)"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-10-05T11:02:34Z","updated_at":"2024-10-05T23:26:26Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"MWE:

```julia
julia> let; 
           function a()
               b()
           end
           function b()
               b = 1
               return b
           end
           @show a()
           @show a()
       end
a() = 1
ERROR: MethodError: objects of type Int64 are not callable
The object of type `Int64` exists, but no method is defined for this combination of argument types when trying to treat it as a callable object.
Maybe you forgot to use an operator such as *, ^, %, / etc. ?
Stacktrace:
 [1] (::var"#a#a##6")()
   @ Main ./REPL[9]:3
 [2] top-level scope
   @ REPL[9]:10
 [3] macro expansion
   @ show.jl:1229 [inlined]
```

As far as I can tell, what's going on is that `a()` and `b()` end up sharing the closed-over `b` in `b()`; either that, or the assignment in `b()` ends up overwriting the binding to `b` in the `let` block, after which the call to `b()` in `a()` fails due to the new object not being a function.

I'd expect this to behave the same as without the `let` block, that is, just print `a() = 1` twice. This was found by a user of Supposition.jl, downstream issue is https://github.com/Seelengrab/Supposition.jl/issues/54. To be clear, the shadowing in `b()` is fine, it's just surprising/unexpected/undesirable to have that shadowing influence what happens in `a()` (which shouldn't care about the shadowing in `b`, and just call the function).

Simpler example:

```julia-repl
julia> let
           function b() 
               b = 1 
               return b
           end 
           b() 
           b
       end
1
```

The `function` expression, when not at module scope, defines a reassignable name.

Perhaps a candidate for strict mode? Xref #54903.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2395225032/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

requests"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-10-05T23:42:31Z","updated_at":"2024-10-06T09:55:23Z","closed_at":"2024-10-06T05:04:58Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently prefixes of existing options are permitted on the CLI, so `--startup` works for `--startup-file`. 

Partial matching constrains the ways the CLI can be modified in the future. For example any prefix of a current option cannot safely be made a new option in case it takes over the behavior of a script that's using that partial match.

We have tab-completion and short options like `-p` so this kind of shortcut shouldn't be necessary. 

I suggest deprecating and removing partial matching.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56010/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":1,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56010/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"not_planned"}}

itself"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2024-10-05T11:02:34Z","updated_at":"2024-10-06T12:42:30Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"MWE:

```julia
julia> let; 
           function a()
               b()
           end
           function b()
               b = 1
               return b
           end
           @show a()
           @show a()
       end
a() = 1
ERROR: MethodError: objects of type Int64 are not callable
The object of type `Int64` exists, but no method is defined for this combination of argument types when trying to treat it as a callable object.
Maybe you forgot to use an operator such as *, ^, %, / etc. ?
Stacktrace:
 [1] (::var"#a#a##6")()
   @ Main ./REPL[9]:3
 [2] top-level scope
   @ REPL[9]:10
 [3] macro expansion
   @ show.jl:1229 [inlined]
```

As far as I can tell, what's going on is that `a()` and `b()` end up sharing the closed-over `b` in `b()`; either that, or the assignment in `b()` ends up overwriting the binding to `b` in the `let` block, after which the call to `b()` in `a()` fails due to the new object not being a function.

I'd expect this to behave the same as without the `let` block, that is, just print `a() = 1` twice. This was found by a user of Supposition.jl, downstream issue is https://github.com/Seelengrab/Supposition.jl/issues/54. To be clear, the shadowing in `b()` is fine, it's just surprising/unexpected/undesirable to have that shadowing influence what happens in `a()` (which shouldn't care about the shadowing in `b`, and just call the function).

requests"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":9,"created_at":"2014-03-13T11:55:17Z","updated_at":"2024-10-06T20:28:57Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"As I was enjoying the entertaining read that is the [IEEE-754 standard for floating point arithmetic](http://ieeexplore.ieee.org/servlet/opac?punumber=4610933), I noticed they recommend some functions (section 9.2), of which we currently lack:
- [ ] exp2m1: `2^x-1`
- [ ] exp10m1: `10^x-1`
- [ ] log2p1: `log2(1+x)` (might want to call this `log21p`, to match `log1p`?)
- [ ] log10p1: `log10(1+x)` (similarly, `log101p`?)
- [ ] rsqrt: `1/sqrt(x)`
- [ ] compound: `(1+x)^n` (`pow1p`?)
- [ ] rootn: `x^(1/n)`
- [ ] various inverse trig-pi functions, e.g. atanpi: `atan(x)/pi` (somewhat confusingly, not all of these are mentioned in the table, although they are referred to in the text below).

There's also three different "pow" functions: `pown` for integer exponents, two for handling floating-point exponents `pow` and `powr` which seem to differ mostly in how signals are raised, so we could probably wait until #2976 is completed.
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/6148/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/6148/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2395574785","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/6148#issuecomment-2395574785","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/6148","id":2395574785,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6OyZIB","user":{"login":"nsajko","id":4944410,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ5NDQ0MTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4944410?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko","html_url":"https://github.com/nsajko","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-06T20:28:56Z","updated_at":"2024-10-06T20:28:56Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Should all of these go into `Base`?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2395574785/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

new feature / enhancement requests"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":11,"created_at":"2019-08-12T23:04:02Z","updated_at":"2024-10-06T20:29:28Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"from IEEE 754-2019: sec 9.2 Additional mathematical operations

"Language standards should define, to be implemented according to this subclause, as many of the operations in Table 9.1 as is appropriate to the language. As with other operations of this standard, the names of the operations in Table 9.1 do not necessarily correspond to the names that any particular programming language would use. In this subclause the domain of an operation is that subset of the affinely extended reals for which the corresponding mathematical function is well defined. A conforming operation shall return results correctly rounded for the applicable rounding direction for all operands in its domain."


_these functions include_
```
rsqrt(x) ≝ 1 / sqrt(x)
compound(x, n) ≝ (1 + x)^n

rootn(x, n) ≝ x^(1/n)
pown(x,n) ≝ x^n
pow(x, y) ≝ x^y [-Inf, +Inf] x [-Inf, +Inf] 
powr(x,y) ≝ x^y [0,+Inf] x [-Inf, +Inf]

exp2m1(x)  ≝ 2^x - 1
exp10m1(x) ≝ 10^x - 1

log2p1(x)  ≝ llog2(1+x)
log10p1(x) ≝ log10(1+x)


sinpi(x) ≝ sin(π * x)
cospi(x) ≝ cos(π * x)
tanpi(x) ≝ tan(π * x)

asinpi(x) ≝ asin(x) / π
acospi(x) ≝ acos(x) / π
atanpi(x) ≝ atan(x) / π

atan2pi(y, x) is the angle subtended at the origin by the point (x, y)
    and the positive x-axis. The range of atan2pi is [−1, +1].
```
there are short tables of required `y = f(x) where x  in {±0, ±∞}`
for many of these functions

```
getpayload(x)
    if the source operand is a NaN, the result is the payload
        as a non-negative floating-point integer.
    if the source operand is not a NaN, the result is −1.

setpayload(x)
    If the source operand is a non-negative floating-point integer whose value
        is one of an implementation-defined set of admissible payloads for
        the operation, the result is a quiet NaN with that payload. 
    Otherwise, the result is +0, with a preferred exponent of 0.

setpayloadsignaling(x)
    like setpayload, the result is a signaling NaN

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56002","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56002/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56002/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56002/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56002","id":2567866417,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6ZDogx","number":56002,"title":"Shadowing in closures in `let` blocks influences bindings in neighboring functions","user":{"login":"Seelengrab","id":11753998,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNzUzOTk4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11753998?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab","html_url":"https://github.com/Seelengrab","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":1363386,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMzYzMzg2","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/breaking","name":"breaking","color":"802000","default":false,"description":"This change will break code"},{"id":371587497,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzE1ODc0OTc=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/design","name":"design","color":"e99695","default":false,"description":"Design of APIs or of the language itself"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":9,"created_at":"2024-10-05T11:02:34Z","updated_at":"2024-10-07T06:02:08Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"MWE:

```julia
julia> let; 
           function a()
               b()
           end
           function b()
               b = 1
               return b
           end
           @show a()
           @show a()
       end
a() = 1
ERROR: MethodError: objects of type Int64 are not callable
The object of type `Int64` exists, but no method is defined for this combination of argument types when trying to treat it as a callable object.
Maybe you forgot to use an operator such as *, ^, %, / etc. ?
Stacktrace:
 [1] (::var"#a#a##6")()
   @ Main ./REPL[9]:3
 [2] top-level scope
   @ REPL[9]:10
 [3] macro expansion
   @ show.jl:1229 [inlined]
```

As far as I can tell, what's going on is that `a()` and `b()` end up sharing the closed-over `b` in `b()`; either that, or the assignment in `b()` ends up overwriting the binding to `b` in the `let` block, after which the call to `b()` in `a()` fails due to the new object not being a function.

I'd expect this to behave the same as without the `let` block, that is, just print `a() = 1` twice. This was found by a user of Supposition.jl, downstream issue is https://github.com/Seelengrab/Supposition.jl/issues/54. To be clear, the shadowing in `b()` is fine, it's just surprising/unexpected/undesirable to have that shadowing influence what happens in `a()` (which shouldn't care about the shadowing in `b`, and just call the function).
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56002/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56002/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2395976757","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56002#issuecomment-2395976757","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56002","id":2395976757,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6Oz7Q1","user":{"login":"nsajko","id":4944410,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ5NDQ0MTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4944410?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko","html_url":"https://github.com/nsajko","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-07T06:02:07Z","updated_at":"2024-10-07T06:02:07Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> introducing a new global scope would be fine

        return
    end
catch err
finally
    push!(ts, 2)
end

was a regression between the last release candidate and the release:

```none
$ hyperfine './julia-1.10.5/bin/julia --startup-file=no -e "1+1"'
Benchmark 1: ./julia-1.10.5/bin/julia --startup-file=no -e "1+1"
  Time (mean ± σ):     160.0 ms ±   0.9 ms    [User: 77.7 ms, System: 84.9 ms]
  Range (min … max):   158.2 ms … 161.3 ms    18 runs
 
$ hyperfine './julia-1.11.0-rc4/bin/julia --startup-file=no -e "1+1"'
Benchmark 1: ./julia-1.11.0-rc4/bin/julia --startup-file=no -e "1+1"
  Time (mean ± σ):     126.1 ms ±   1.6 ms    [User: 61.7 ms, System: 63.3 ms]
  Range (min … max):   122.8 ms … 128.6 ms    23 runs
 
$ hyperfine './julia-1.11.0/bin/julia --startup-file=no -e "1+1"'
Benchmark 1: ./julia-1.11.0/bin/julia --startup-file=no -e "1+1"
  Time (mean ± σ):     304.6 ms ±   4.4 ms    [User: 528.1 ms, System: 84.3 ms]
  Range (min … max):   300.0 ms … 315.1 ms    10 runs
```

Maybe because of this additional required compilation:

```none
$ ./julia-1.11.0-rc4/bin/julia --startup-file=no -e "1+1" --trace-compile=stderr
$ ./julia-1.11.0/bin/julia --startup-file=no -e "1+1" --trace-compile=stderr
precompile(Tuple{Base.var"#759#760"{Task}})
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56063/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56063/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"opened","number":56095,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/56095","id":2118340816,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5-Q1DQ","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56095","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56095.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56095.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56095","number":56095,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Base.MPFR: avoid reading the global defaults unless necessary","user":{"login":"nsajko","id":4944410,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ5NDQ0MTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4944410?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko","html_url":"https://github.com/nsajko","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Another follow-up to #51362.

Make sure the global default precision and rounding mode are only dereferenced when necessary (when there's no relevant scope, in the ScopedValues sense).

Currently this change doesn't result in performance improvements, presumably due to how costly the access to a `ScopedValue` currently is, but the idea is to avoid the cost of the dereference when possible.

Once ScopedValues are better optimized by the compiler, I guess this would also result in better effects in case it's known that a call is within a ScopedValues ```julia
julia> f(1) = 2
ERROR: syntax: "1" is not a valid function argument name around REPL[8]:1
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[8]:1
 [2] top-level scope
   @ none:1
```

`Array` of `SomeUserType`.  E.g.:

```julia
julia> struct D

> Base.getproperty(A::AbstractArray{<:D}, f::Symbol) = getindex.(getfield.(A, :dict), f)
> ```

This is type piracy, for the `getproperty(::AbstractArray{Union{}}, ::Symbol)` type signature. That particular issue is easily fixable, like this (just remove the `<:`):

```julia
Base.getproperty(A::AbstractArray{D}, f::Symbol) = getindex.(getfield.(A, :dict), f)
```

Even so, this is bound to cause ambiguity errors as soon as the `AbstractArray` has their own `getproperty`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2406643551/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

dc689fe8700f70f4a4e2dbaaf270f26b87e79e04 tries to remove the slow `may_contain_union_decision` check by re-organizing the code path. Now the fast path has been removed and most of its optimization has been integrated into the preserved slow path.
Since the slow path stores all inner ∃ decisions on the outer most R stack, there might be overflow risk.
aee69a41441b4306ba3ee5e845bc96cb45d9b327 should fix that concern.

The reported MWE now becomes
```julia
  0.000002 seconds
  0.000040 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 52.00% compilation time)
  0.000023 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 49.36% compilation time)
  0.000026 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 50.38% compilation time)
  0.000027 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 54.95% compilation time)
  0.000019 seconds (106 allocations: 4.922 KiB, 49.73% compilation time)
  0.000024 seconds (105 allocations: 4.828 KiB, 52.24% compilation time)
```

Local bench also shows that 72855cd slightly accelerates `OmniPackage.jl`'s loading
```julia
julia> @time using OmniPackage
 20.525278 seconds (25.36 M allocations: 1.606 GiB, 8.48% gc time, 12.89% compilation time: 77% of which was recompilation)

Fricas, at least, is much more general than that (really, check it out, it's a beautiful system/programming language, it comes from an old lineage but sometimes very similar to Julia, with a thoughtfully designed standard library which the Julia world could possibly learn something from). From a quick look, what they do is this:
* implement the binary `lcm` for **any GCD domain** (generically) in terms of binary `gcd`
    * https://github.com/fricas/fricas/blob/5a7a56aab971354823c20eace6d532396dd8a176/src/algebra/catdef.spad#L723-L728
    * Wikipedia page for GCD domain
        * https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GCD_domain
        * > In mathematics, a GCD domain (sometimes called just domain) is an [integral domain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Integral_domain) R with the property that any two elements have a [greatest common divisor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greatest_common_divisor) (GCD); i.e., there is a unique minimal [principal ideal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Principal_ideal) containing the ideal [generated by](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subring#Subring_generated_by_a_set) two given elements. Equivalently, any two elements of R have a least common multiple (LCM).
* implement the n-ary (list) `lcm` for **any GCD domain** in terms of `reduce` and the binary `lcm`
    * https://github.com/fricas/fricas/blob/5a7a56aab971354823c20eace6d532396dd8a176/src/algebra/catdef.spad#L729-L729
    * `1` is chosen as the `init` (in Julian terms) for the `reduce`. I think this makes sense analogously to how the multiplicative identity is chosen as the value of the empty product - it's (more or less) also the identity element for `lcm`.
* implement the n-ary (list) `gcd` for **any GCD domain** in terms of `reduce` and the binary `gcd`
    * https://github.com/fricas/fricas/blob/5a7a56aab971354823c20eace6d532396dd8a176/src/algebra/catdef.spad#L730-L730
    * `0` is chosen as the `init` (in Julian terms) for the `reduce`. I think this makes sense analogously to how the additive identity is chosen as the value of the empty sum - it's (more or less) also the identity element for `gcd`.
* implement the binary `gcd` for various structures that are GCD domains, e.g., Euclidean domains or fields
    * http://fricas.github.io/search.html?q=gcddomain
    * `gcd` for fields: https://github.com/fricas/fricas/blob/5a7a56aab971354823c20eace6d532396dd8a176/src/algebra/catdef.spad#L600-L602
    * `gcd` for Euclidean domains: https://github.com/fricas/fricas/blob/5a7a56aab971354823c20eace6d532396dd8a176/src/algebra/catdef.spad#L488-L499","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2408251911/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

b.com/users/nsajko/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-12T10:24:52Z","updated_at":"2024-10-12T10:24:52Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Here's a script demonstrating the win here:

```julia
const new_type_count = 10000;

for i ∈ 1:new_type_count
    @eval struct $(Symbol(:S, i)) end
end

const tup_ta = let f
    function f(i::Int)
        new_object = (@eval $(Symbol(:S, i)))()
        (new_object, nothing)
    end
    ntuple(f, new_type_count)
end;

const tup_fr = let f
    function f(i::Int)
        new_object = (@eval $(Symbol(:S, i)))()
        (nothing, new_object)
    end
    ntuple(f, new_type_count)
end;

const ta = Base.tail;
const fr = Base.front;

@timev for i ∈ eachindex(tup_ta)
    ta(tup_ta[i])
end

@timev for i ∈ eachindex(tup_fr)
    fr(tup_fr[i])
end

VERSION
```

Nightly:

library"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-06-29T15:44:32Z","updated_at":"2024-10-12T17:41:07Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"See also https://github.com/JuliaDocs/Documenter.jl/issues/2160

```julia
julia> const m = Module()
Main.anonymous

julia> @eval m begin
           abstract type Foo end
           struct Bar <: Foo end
       end

julia> supertype(m.Bar) === m.Foo
true

julia> subtypes(m.Foo)
Any[]
```

`subtypes` should  obviously contain `m.Bar` as well. Whether the module is anonymous or not doesn't Recently I discovered that hashing higher-dimensional arrays is faster when flatting them first.

```julia
using BenchmarkTools

@benchmark hash($rand(512)) # 8.072 μs
@benchmark hash($rand(8,8,8)) # 26.493 μs
@benchmark hash(vec($rand(8,8,8))) # 8.239 μs 
```

change","color":"a05255","default":false,"description":"Marginal behavior change acceptable for a minor release"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-10-14T22:51:53Z","updated_at":"2024-10-14T22:51:53Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Regarding the greatest common divisor (GCD) and least common multiple (LCM), as binary operators, it seems that two incompatible meanings exist:
* The meaning used in Euclid's geometrical text (Elements), based on commensurability
    * An issue with this meaning, for Julian purposes, is that it's not defined when all arguments are zero.
    * As used, for example, here:
        * https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/151081/gcd-of-rationals
        * https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/122307/gcd-of-any-two-real-numbers
* A, more modern, abstract algebraic meaning
    * An issue with this meaning, for Julian purposes, is that the values are trivial for any field, such as the rational numbers, for example. Rational numbers are supported for Julia's `gcd` and `lcm` for a long time, and changes here would be breaking and quite disruptive.
    * As used, for example, here:
        * https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/85565/motivation-behind-the-definition-of-gcd-and-lcm
        * https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/4145760/gcd-of-rational-and-irrational-numbers
    * As used in the standard library of [Fricas](https://fricas.github.io/)
        * https://github.com/fricas/fricas/issues/183

@cyanescent and @barucden point out in #55379 and #56113 that:
* `lcm` is currently broken for arrays of nonintegral numbers
* the breakage stems from a faulty, non-identity, `init` argument to reduce (the `lcm` for array arguments is implemented in terms of `reduce` and the binary `lcm`)

@cyanescent further points out in the linked PR that perhaps a valid value for `init` doesn't exist. It seems to me that @cyanescent is correct and the attempt at supporting empty collections is misguided, because it corresponds to the second meaning above, while Julia otherwise uses the first meaning above.

The `init` arguments should be removed, I guess, while breaking support for empty arrays, as per @barucden's suggestion in the linked issue. ```julia
using BenchmarkTools

function rle1(str)
    i = 1
    n = length(str)

    while i < n + 1
        i += 1
    end
end

function rle2(str)
    i = 1

    while i < length(str) + 1
        i += 1
    end
end

str = 'A'^1000

println(@benchmark rle1(str))
println(@benchmark rle2(str))
```

Result is below.

```console
yuu@penguin:~/src/wakka$ julia measure.jl
Trial(1.497 μs)
Trial(1.492 ms)
```

## Expected behavior

rle1() and rle2() take the same amount of time.

## Actual behavior

rle2() is slower than rle1().

## System configuration

    end
    i
end

does not provide a function for checking divisibility:
```julia
"""
    isdivisible(m, n)

Return true if `m` is divisible by `n`.
"""
isdivisible(m::Integer, n::Integer)
```

It can be implemented as `iszero(rem(m, n))` but that's not the optimal implementation for all types from Base (it allocates for a `BigInt`). 

AbstractAlgebra.jl from Nemo implements this exact function: https://github.com/Nemocas/AbstractAlgebra.jl/blob/396fdddbc1d1620a7b2866e7340e39b14228176e/src/julia/Integer.jl#L121-L151
I would like to have it in Base.

functions"},{"id":1056009044,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMDU2MDA5MDQ0","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/minor%20change","name":"minor change","color":"a05255","default":false,"description":"Marginal behavior change acceptable for a minor release"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-10-14T22:51:53Z","updated_at":"2024-10-18T11:46:47Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Regarding the greatest common divisor (GCD) and least common multiple (LCM), as binary operators, it seems that two incompatible meanings exist:
* The meaning used in Euclid's geometrical text (Elements), based on commensurability
    * An issue with this meaning, for Julian purposes, is that it's not defined when all arguments are zero.
    * As used, for example, here:
        * https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/151081/gcd-of-rationals
        * https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/122307/gcd-of-any-two-real-numbers
* A, more modern, abstract algebraic meaning
    * An issue with this meaning, for Julian purposes, is that the values are trivial for any field, such as the rational numbers, for example. Rational numbers are supported for Julia's `gcd` and `lcm` for a long time, and changes here would be breaking and quite disruptive.
    * As used, for example, here:
        * https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/85565/motivation-behind-the-definition-of-gcd-and-lcm
        * https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/4145760/gcd-of-rational-and-irrational-numbers
    * As used in the standard library of [Fricas](https://fricas.github.io/)
        * https://github.com/fricas/fricas/issues/183

@cyanescent and @barucden point out in #55379 and #56113 that:
* `lcm` is currently broken for arrays of nonintegral numbers
* the breakage stems from a faulty, non-identity, `init` argument to reduce (the `lcm` for array arguments is implemented in terms of `reduce` and the binary `lcm`)

@cyanescent further points out in the linked PR that perhaps a valid value for `init` doesn't exist. It seems to me that @cyanescent is correct and the attempt at supporting empty collections is misguided, because it corresponds to the second meaning above, while Julia otherwise uses the first meaning above.

The `init` arguments should be removed, I guess, while breaking support for empty arrays, as per @barucden's suggestion in the linked issue. I'll try to guide @cyanescent in forming their PR to fix this (mostly just tests are necessary). Also I guess a PkgEval will be requests"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":15,"created_at":"2024-10-17T12:22:43Z","updated_at":"2024-10-19T09:37:42Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Base does not provide a function for checking divisibility:
```julia
"""
    isdivisible(m, n)

Return true if `m` is divisible by `n`.
"""
isdivisible(m::Integer, n::Integer)
```

It can be implemented as `iszero(rem(m, n))` but that's not the optimal implementation for all types from Base (it allocates for a `BigInt`). 

AbstractAlgebra.jl from Nemo implements this exact function: https://github.com/Nemocas/AbstractAlgebra.jl/blob/396fdddbc1d1620a7b2866e7340e39b14228176e/src/julia/Integer.jl#L121-L151
I would like to have it in Base.

(Discussed on Discourse: https://discourse.julialang.org/t/checking-divisibility-efficiently/121418)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56212/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56212/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2423722026","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56212#issuecomment-2423722026","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56212","id":2423722026,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6QdxAq","user":{"login":"nsajko","id":4944410,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ5NDQ0MTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4944410?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko","html_url":"https://github.com/nsajko","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-19T09:37:41Z","updated_at":"2024-10-19T09:37:41Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> AbstractAlgebra.jl from Nemo implements this exact function

change catches the overflow in 
```julia
(Int8(1)//Int8(1)) / (Int8(100) + Int8(100)im)
```
instead of incorrectly returning 
```julia
25//8 - 25//8*im
```

This PR removes some of the specific `/` methods that involve complex rationals in `rational.jl`. 
The default methods in `complex.jl` have better promotion rules and edge case checking. This includes the checks added in #32050, fixing #23134 for rational numbers.

The previous dispatch to `//` ignored overflow errors (see #53435), but would return the correct answer if the numerator was zero even if the denominator would overflow.

docstring for `filesize` failes to mention how paths are joined, and document the full-path-as-single-argument method. is the method:

https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/877de9839809e769d4f9707a61df3400d087d8d3/base/rational.jl#L101-L101

The math behind the transformation incorrectly assumes `y` is finite and nonzero.

xref #53435, which concerns overflow caused by the same oneliner.

Test:

```julia
using Test

@testset "exact division by a zero or infinite complex number" begin
    for y ∈ (0, 1 // 0, -1 // 0)
        @test (7 // complex(y)) == (7 // y)
    end
end
```

Resulting in:

```none
Test Summary:                                       | Error  Total  Time
exact division by a zero or infinite complex number |     3      3  1.6s
ERROR: Some tests did not pass: 0 passed, 0 failed, 3 errored, 0 broken.
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56245/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56245/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

throwing"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-02-22T15:28:46Z","updated_at":"2024-10-19T16:57:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The current method for `//` on `Integer` and `Complex{<:Integer}` can overflow, silently giving an incorrect answer. For example:

```julia
julia> Int8(8) // Int8(100)im
0//1 - 50//1*im
```
The expected answer is `0//1 - 2//25*im`

The issue is caused by `abs2` and `conj` on `Complex{Int8}` silently overflowing in:
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/ccba6c91e24ebc0940acc2f8c390e6391ce0e292/base/rational.jl#L101

One solution could be to specialize this method on the many different combinations of `Complex{<:Rational}`, `Complex{<:Integer}`, `Rational`, `Integer`, and `Number` for the inputs, and check for overflow when doing any `Integer` math. Maybe there is a nicer way to fix this issue.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53435/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53435/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2424083364","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53435#issuecomment-2424083364","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53435","id":2424083364,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6QfJOk","user":{"login":"nsajko","id":4944410,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ5NDQ0MTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4944410?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko","html_url":"https://github.com/nsajko","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-19T16:57:25Z","updated_at":"2024-10-19T16:57:25Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"The offending oneliner is buggy in other ways, too: #56245.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2424083364/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

change catches the overflow in 
```julia
(Int8(1)//Int8(1)) / (Int8(100) + Int8(100)im)
```
instead of incorrectly returning 
```julia
25//8 - 25//8*im
```

This PR removes some of the specific `/` methods that involve complex rationals in `rational.jl`. 
The default methods in `complex.jl` have better promotion rules and edge case checking. This includes the checks added in #32050, fixing #23134 for rational numbers.

The previous dispatch to `//` ignored overflow errors (see #53435), but would return the correct answer if the numerator was zero even if the denominator would overflow.

change catches the overflow in 
```julia
(Int8(1)//Int8(1)) / (Int8(100) + Int8(100)im)
```
instead of incorrectly returning 
```julia
25//8 - 25//8*im
```

This PR removes some of the specific `/` methods that involve complex rationals in `rational.jl`. 
The default methods in `complex.jl` have better promotion rules and edge case checking. This includes the checks added in #32050, fixing #23134 for rational numbers.

The previous dispatch to `//` ignored overflow errors (see #53435), but would return the correct answer if the numerator was zero even if the denominator would overflow.

following code crashes Julia
```julia

end
```

https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/35067

It's faster for some complicated Real types.
```julia
julia> f(x) = x<=zero(typeof(x))
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> g(x) = signbit(x)||iszero(x)
g (generic function with 1 method)

julia> @btime f(x) setup=(x=rand(BigFloat))
  99.055 ns (4 allocations: 144 bytes)
true

julia> @btime g(x) setup=(x=rand(BigFloat))
  10.700 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
false
```

If you think this is a good idea then I'll add docstrings and tests.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53677/reactions","total_count":3,"+1":2,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":1,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53677/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2427236792","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53677#issuecomment-2427236792","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53677","id":2427236792,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6QrLG4","user":{"login":"nsajko","id":4944410,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ5NDQ0MTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4944410?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko","html_url":"https://github.com/nsajko","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nsajko/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-21T16:55:09Z","updated_at":"2024-10-21T16:55:09Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Tests are failing:

https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/35067

It's faster for some complicated Real types.
```julia
julia> f(x) = x<=zero(typeof(x))
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> g(x) = signbit(x)||iszero(x)
g (generic function with 1 method)

julia> @btime f(x) setup=(x=rand(BigFloat))
  99.055 ns (4 allocations: 144 bytes)
true

julia> @btime g(x) setup=(x=rand(BigFloat))
  10.700 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
false
```

 signbit(x::Real) = x < 0
 
+"""
+    ispositive(x)
+
+Test whether `x > 0`. See also [`isnegative`](@ref).
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.12"
+    This function requires at least Julia 1.12.
+
+# Examples

It's faster for some complicated Real types.
```julia
julia> f(x) = x<=zero(typeof(x))
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> g(x) = signbit(x)||iszero(x)
g (generic function with 1 method)

julia> @btime f(x) setup=(x=rand(BigFloat))
  99.055 ns (4 allocations: 144 bytes)
true

julia> @btime g(x) setup=(x=rand(BigFloat))
  10.700 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
false
```

https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/35067

It's faster for some complicated Real types.
```julia
julia> f(x) = x<=zero(typeof(x))
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> g(x) = signbit(x)||iszero(x)
g (generic function with 1 method)

julia> @btime f(x) setup=(x=rand(BigFloat))
  99.055 ns (4 allocations: 144 bytes)
true

julia> @btime g(x) setup=(x=rand(BigFloat))
  10.700 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
false
```

+julia> ispositive(-4.0)
+false
+
+julia> ispositive(99)
+true
+
+julia> ispositive(0.0)
+false
+```

It's faster for some complicated Real types.
```julia
julia> f(x) = x<=zero(typeof(x))
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> g(x) = signbit(x)||iszero(x)
g (generic function with 1 method)

  99.055 ns (4 allocations: 144 bytes)
true

julia> @btime g(x) setup=(x=rand(BigFloat))
  10.700 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
false
```

If you think this is a good idea then I'll add docstrings and tests.

Edit: FAQs according to discussions below
- Why `ispositive(x::Bool) = x`?
  - Because it's fast. https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53677#discussion_r1530731988
- Why support `ispositive(::Any)`?
  - #35067

It's faster for some complicated Real types.
```julia
julia> f(x) = x<=zero(typeof(x))
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> g(x) = signbit(x)||iszero(x)
g (generic function with 1 method)

julia> @btime f(x) setup=(x=rand(BigFloat))
  99.055 ns (4 allocations: 144 bytes)
true

julia> @btime g(x) setup=(x=rand(BigFloat))
  10.700 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
false
```

If you think this is a good idea then I'll add docstrings and tests.

Edit: FAQs according to discussions below
- Why `ispositive(x::Bool) = x`?
  - Because it's fast. https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53677#discussion_r1530731988
- Why support `ispositive(::Any)`?
  - #35067
